corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF for Clinically Significant Incidental Findings Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses de-identified chest CT reports and metadata from a hospital system spanning 4–5 years. The raw dataset comprises approximately 50,000 studies; a subset of ~10,000 is labeled by linking reports to downstream pathology results, follow-up imaging, or clinician adjudication within 12 months of the initial scan. A study is labeled positive if follow-up confirmed the incidental finding was clinically significant (required intervention or close monitoring); studies with benign or no follow-up findings are labeled negative. Crucially, only studies with documented follow-up disposition are retained; unlabeled studies are excluded to avoid training the model on false negatives (findings that were never investigated because they were unreported or clinically dismissed).

This labeling approach introduces class imbalance (~20% positive) and selective labeling bias, both of which are realistic to the deployment setting. These are addressed through evaluation metrics (AUPRC, calibration) and stratification in the split strategy, not by rebalancing the training data.

### 2. Temporal and Site-Stratified Split

To prevent data leakage of reporting language evolution and clinical protocol drift, the data is split chronologically: training (months 1–36, ~6,000 labeled studies), validation (months 37–42, ~2,000 studies), test (months 43–48, ~2,000 studies). Within each temporal window, the data is stratified by imaging site and scanner model, ensuring each site appears proportionally in all three sets. This preserves the realistic distribution of multi-site variation while preventing future information from corrupting past training data.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The model operates on full radiology report text (impression, findings, and technical sections). Preprocessing transformers — including text tokenization, a fitted vocabulary, and any scaling or imputation for structured features — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data without refitting.

Engineered features include: (1) contextual embeddings from a pretrained clinical BERT model (e.g., ClinicalBERT), fine-tuned on training data; (2) structured metadata (one-hot encoded scanner model, imaging site, report length, acquisition day-of-week); (3) no features encoding the label, follow-up date, or future clinical events. Text is lowercased and whitespace-tokenized without aggressive stemming to preserve clinical terminology.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model fine-tunes a pretrained clinical language model by appending a two-layer feedforward classifier (hidden dimension 256, dropout 0.2) on the [CLS] token embedding, trained end-to-end with cross-entropy loss on the training set.

The baseline is a bag-of-words TF-IDF classifier: TF-IDF features (max 5,000 terms, min document frequency 5) extracted from reports and fit on training data, followed by logistic regression with L2 regularization. Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (regularization strength C) are selected via grid search on validation data, and the baseline uses the same structured metadata features as the transformer model. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of learned embeddings, not differences in data or compute.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

Primary metric: AUROC on the held-out test set. AUROC is chosen because clinicians must rank-order studies by risk to set safe triage thresholds; AUROC measures discrimination across all thresholds and is robust to class imbalance and operational flexibility.

Secondary metrics: (1) expected calibration error — clinicians must trust model confidence; (2) sensitivity at 90% specificity — operationally relevant alert rate; (3) precision-recall AUC — emphasizes performance on sparse positive class; (4) site-stratified AUROC — rules out site-specific confounding.

Model selection uses validation AUROC; the test set is held untouched until final evaluation and is evaluated exactly once. Confidence intervals (95% via bootstrap resampling) are reported on all test metrics.

### 6. Confound Controls

Temporal splitting prevents leakage of evolving reporting and clinical protocols. Site stratification across all splits ensures generalization across scanners and institutions. Structured metadata are included as explicit features so that site effects are learned rather than hidden. On test data, AUROC is computed separately by site; variation >5 percentage points triggers investigation of site-specific artifacts. An independent clinician re-adjudicates 200 random test labels to quantify label noise. An ablation study (on test data) compares baseline performance with and without structured metadata, confirming that site features are not driving the model gap.

These controls do not establish that the model learns true clinical reasoning, but they confirm technical soundness and external validity across sites and time.
